suggested.title: [Observe Together] A magnifying glass or hand lens

competence_markers.description: The student can identify what living things need to survive and give examples from direct observation
The student has observed at least one complete life cycle and can describe the stages
The student can construct a food chain or food web from organisms in their local environment and explain the connections
The student can describe major human body systems by function and explain why each matters
The student understands that microorganisms exist, that most are not harmful, and can name helpful examples
The student maintains biological observations in their nature journal with drawings, descriptions, and dated entries
The student demonstrates care for living things — plants or animals — with understanding of their biological needs
The student notices and records seasonal biological patterns in their environment

narration_prompts.detail: How is a tree different from a rock? A fish different from a river?

narration_prompts.prompt: What do all living things need to survive?
narration_prompts.detail: Give examples from plants and animals you have observed.

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about the food web in your yard or neighborhood.
narration_prompts.detail: Who eats whom? What would happen if you removed one organism?

narration_prompts.prompt: Pick a body system and explain what it does.
narration_prompts.detail: Why does your heart beat? Why do you need to breathe? Where does your food go after you eat it?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about the plant you grew from a seed.
narration_prompts.detail: What happened at each stage? What did it need? What happened when it did not get what it needed?
Are they all bad? Name some that help you or help the world.

narration_prompts.prompt: What changes have you noticed in nature across the seasons?

cross_connections.description: Nature observation, the organism study, and the plant growth experiment all apply scientific thinking skills directly.
Cooking and fermentation connect chemistry to biology. The chemical changes in food are biological processes.
The garden is where biology and food production meet. The student growing plants is practicing both biology and agriculture.
Understanding what living things need — water, shelter, energy — connects to survival skills.
Understanding body systems (muscles, bones, heart, lungs) connects directly to physical performance.
Understanding nutrition as biology connects cooking to health.
Everything in this packet is the experiential foundation for the cellular and systems-level biology of Tier B1.

content.why_this_matters:
You are a living thing. So is the tree in your yard, the bird outside your window, the worm in the soil, and the bacteria on your skin that you cannot see. Every living thing — from the smallest bacterium to the largest whale — must solve the same basic problems: getting energy, growing, staying alive, and making more of itself.

In this packet, you will study life as it actually is — not from diagrams in a book, but from observation of real organisms. You will watch seeds sprout and grow. You will observe animals and learn what they need. You will discover that all living things depend on other living things, connected in webs of feeding and need. And you will begin to understand your own body — not as an abstract diagram but as a living machine that you are responsible for maintaining.

The best biology education at your age happens outside: in the garden, by the pond, in the woods, watching ants on the sidewalk, observing birds at a feeder. Your nature journal from 1.6.1 is your primary biology tool. Everything you observe about living things is biology.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Understanding of what living things need** — water, energy, air, shelter — observed in real organisms
2. **Life cycle awareness** — seeds to plants, eggs to animals, birth to death — observed through real growth
3. **Habitat and interdependence** — the understanding that organisms depend on each other in food chains and food webs
4. **Basic human body systems** — the heart, lungs, stomach, bones, muscles, brain, and skin understood through function, not memorization
5. **Microorganism awareness** — the understanding that the world is full of organisms too small to see, and that most are not harmful
6. **Direct observation of life** — the habit of studying living things carefully, patiently, and over time
content.core_content:
### Part 1: What Makes Something Alive?

How do you know if something is alive? A rock is not alive. A tree is. A river is not alive (though it contains living things). A fish is.

Living things share certain qualities:
- **They need energy.** Plants get energy from sunlight. Animals get energy from food.
- **They grow.** A seed becomes a tree. A kitten becomes a cat. A baby becomes an adult.
- **They respond to their environment.** A plant turns toward light. A rabbit runs from a hawk. You pull your hand away from something hot.
- **They reproduce.** Plants make seeds. Animals have offspring. Even bacteria divide to make more bacteria.
- **They are made of cells.** Every living thing is built from tiny units called cells — too small to see without a microscope, but they are there.

Not everything that moves is alive (rivers flow, clouds move, fire spreads — but none are alive). Not everything that is still is dead (a hibernating bear is still alive). The qualities of life are not about any single feature — they are about the combination.

### Part 2: What Living Things Need

Every living thing needs certain things to survive:

**Water.** Every living cell needs water. Plants pull water from the soil through their roots. Animals drink it. Without water, all life dies — usually within days for animals, within weeks for most plants.

**Energy.** Plants capture energy from sunlight through a process called photosynthesis — they use sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide from the air to make sugar, which stores the energy. Animals cannot make their own food — they must eat plants or other animals to get energy.

**Air.** Animals breathe oxygen from the air. Plants also use oxygen (and produce it during photosynthesis). Carbon dioxide, which animals breathe out, is what plants breathe in. This is a cycle — animals and plants need each other.

**Shelter and protection.** Birds build nests. Rabbits dig burrows. Trees have bark. Your skin is your body's outer protection.

**Experiment:** Plant a bean seed in a cup of soil. Give it water and light. Plant another bean seed and give it water but no light (keep it in a dark closet). Plant a third and give it light but no water. Observe over two weeks. What does each plant need to grow? What happens without each requirement?

### Part 3: Growth and Life Cycles

Everything alive goes through a life cycle — birth, growth, reproduction, and death.

**Plants:** A seed germinates (sprouts). The root grows down into the soil. The shoot grows up toward the light. The plant grows leaves, which capture sunlight. The plant flowers. The flower is pollinated (often by insects). Seeds form. The seeds fall or are carried away. The cycle begins again.

**Animals:** Born (or hatched). Grows. Matures. Reproduces. Ages. Dies.

Some life cycles are dramatic:
- A caterpillar wraps itself in a chrysalis and emerges as a butterfly — metamorphosis. The same organism, completely transformed.
- A frog begins life as a tadpole — with a tail and gills, living in water. It metamorphoses into a frog — with legs and lungs, living on land.
- A chicken egg, if incubated for 21 days, produces a chick. The transformation from egg to living bird is observable.

**Activity:** If possible, observe a full life cycle directly. Grow a plant from seed to flower to seed. Raise caterpillars into butterflies (kits are available). Hatch chicken eggs (if you have the setup). The direct observation of a complete life cycle is one of the most powerful biological experiences a young student can have.

### Part 4: Food Chains and Food Webs

Every living thing gets its energy from something else.

**Producers** make their own food from sunlight. All green plants are producers.

**Consumers** eat other organisms. Rabbits eat plants (herbivores). Hawks eat rabbits (carnivores). Bears eat plants and animals (omnivores). You are an omnivore.

**Decomposers** break down dead organisms and return nutrients to the soil. Fungi, bacteria, and worms are decomposers. Without them, dead things would pile up and nutrients would be locked away.

A **food chain** shows who eats whom in a line: grass -> rabbit -> hawk.

A **food web** shows the full picture — many food chains connected together, because most organisms eat more than one thing and are eaten by more than one thing.

**The key insight:** Remove one part of the web and the others are affected. If you remove the wolves from an ecosystem, the deer population grows unchecked. The deer overgraze the plants. The plants disappear. The soil erodes. The rivers change course. (This actually happened in Yellowstone National Park, and when wolves were reintroduced, the ecosystem recovered.) Everything is connected.

### Part 5: Your Body — A Machine You Operate

Your body is made of systems that work together to keep you alive. You do not need to memorize all their names. You need to understand what they do.

**Heart and blood (circulatory system):** Your heart pumps blood through a network of tubes (blood vessels) that reaches every part of your body. Blood carries oxygen from your lungs and nutrients from your food to every cell. It also carries waste away. Your heart beats about 100,000 times a day without you thinking about it.

**Lungs (respiratory system):** When you breathe in, air fills your lungs. Oxygen from the air passes into your blood. Carbon dioxide — a waste product of your cells using energy — passes from your blood into your lungs and is breathed out. Every breath is a gas exchange.

**Stomach and intestines (digestive system):** When you eat, your teeth break food into smaller pieces. Your stomach churns it with acid and enzymes that break it down further. Your intestines absorb the nutrients into your blood. What your body cannot use is eliminated as waste.

**Bones (skeletal system):** 206 bones support your body, protect your organs (your skull protects your brain, your ribs protect your heart and lungs), and provide attachment points for muscles.

**Muscles (muscular system):** Muscles contract (shorten) to move your bones. Every movement you make — walking, lifting, writing, breathing — is muscles contracting.

**Brain and nerves (nervous system):** Your brain receives information from your senses, processes it, and sends instructions to your muscles and organs. Your nerves are the wiring that carries these signals throughout your body.

**Skin (integumentary system):** Your skin is your body's largest organ. It protects you from infection, regulates your temperature, and gives you the sense of touch.

### Part 6: Not All Small Things Are Scary

The world is full of organisms too small to see with your eyes. These are **microorganisms** — bacteria, fungi, and other tiny living things.

Most people think of microorganisms as germs — things that make you sick. Some do. But most do not. In fact, most microorganisms are either harmless or helpful:

- **Bacteria in your gut** help you digest food and support your immune system. You have trillions of them — more bacterial cells in your body than human cells.
- **Yeast** (a tiny fungus) makes bread rise and produces the alcohol in beer and wine.
- **Bacteria in soil** break down dead material and make nutrients available to plants.
- **Bacteria in yogurt and cheese** ferment milk into different products.

Some microorganisms do cause disease — the bacteria that cause strep throat, the viruses that cause colds and flu. But understanding microorganisms means understanding that the vast majority are part of the normal, healthy functioning of your body and the natural world. They are not enemies. They are partners.

### Part 7: Seasons and Change

Living things change with the seasons, and observing these changes is one of the most accessible forms of biological study.

**Spring:** Seeds germinate. Trees bud. Flowers bloom. Birds return from migration. Animals that hibernated wake up. Days get longer.

**Summer:** Plants grow rapidly. Animals are active, feeding and raising young. Insects are abundant.

**Fall:** Leaves change color (chlorophyll breaks down, revealing other pigments that were there all along). Animals prepare for winter — some store food, some fatten up, some grow thicker coats. Birds migrate. Days shorten.

**Winter:** Many plants are dormant — alive but not growing. Some animals hibernate (bears, groundhogs). Some remain active (deer, foxes, chickadees). The world seems quieter, but life continues.

**Nature journal practice:** Record seasonal changes in your journal. Over a full year, you will have a record of the biological rhythms of your specific place — a record unique to you and valuable as science.

### Part 8: Caring for Living Things

The student who has a garden, a pet, or access to animals is practicing biology every day.

**Plants:** What does this plant need? How much water? How much light? What soil? When you take care of a garden, you are learning what plants require to live — and what happens when those requirements are not met.

**Animals:** What does this animal eat? How much water does it need? Does it need shelter? Exercise? Social contact? Taking responsibility for an animal is a direct lesson in biology — you must understand the organism's needs to keep it alive and healthy.

This is not just biology education. It is training in responsibility, observation, and the reality that living things depend on competent care.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: The Seed-to-Plant Growth Study [Multi-Week — Core Activity]
Plant several types of seeds (beans, sunflowers, radishes). Observe and measure daily. Record growth in the nature journal with drawings and measurements. Vary conditions for some plants (light/dark, water/no water). This is the primary multi-week biology experiment of the packet.

### Study 2: Animal Observation [Ongoing]
Choose an animal the student can observe regularly — a pet, a bird species at a feeder, a squirrel, an ant colony, a pond organism. Observe behavior over several weeks. Record in the nature journal: what does it eat? When is it active? How does it behave? What patterns do you notice?

### Study 3: Food Chain/Food Web Construction [Mid-Packet]
In the student's local environment, identify at least ten organisms. Connect them in a food web — who eats whom? Identify producers, consumers, and decomposers. Discuss: what would happen if one organism were removed?

### Study 4: Body Systems Exploration [Mid-Packet]
Using the DK Human Body book and direct experience: feel your pulse (circulatory system). Hold your breath and notice the urge to breathe (respiratory system). Eat a cracker and notice the saliva dissolving it (digestive system). Flex your arm and feel the muscle contract (muscular system). The student narrates what each system does and why it matters.

### Study 5: Microorganism Investigation [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Make yogurt or sourdough bread. Discuss: what living organisms are doing the work? Are they harmful? What are they changing about the food? If available, look at pond water under a microscope or magnifying glass. The student discovers that the invisible world is teeming with life.

### Study 6: Seasonal Observation [Ongoing — Full Year If Possible]
Record seasonal biological changes in the nature journal throughout the packet. Bloom dates, migration, leaf color change, animal behavior, insect activity. After several months, review: what patterns did you find? How do living things respond to the changing seasons?

### Study 7: Life Cycle Direct Observation [Multi-Week]
Observe at least one complete life cycle: a plant from seed to seed, a butterfly from caterpillar to adult, a chick from egg to hatching, or a frog from tadpole to adult. The student documents each stage with drawings and descriptions.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Living Things Needs
**Developing well:** Can list what living things need and give examples from organisms they have observed. Understands that plants and animals solve the same problems differently.
**Developing slowly:** Memorized a list but cannot connect it to real organisms.

### 2. Life Cycles
**Developing well:** Has observed at least one complete life cycle and can narrate the stages from direct experience.
**Developing slowly:** Knows that plants grow from seeds but has not observed the process directly.
**Diagnostic:** Plant seeds immediately. Bean seeds germinate in days and provide a fast, visible life cycle.

### 3. Food Webs
**Developing well:** Can identify organisms in their local environment and connect them in a food web. Understands that removing one organism affects others.
**Developing slowly:** Cannot connect organisms to each other. Views living things as isolated.

### 4. Body Systems
**Developing well:** Can explain major body systems by function using their own words and personal experience.
**Developing slowly:** Memorized system names but cannot explain what they do.

### 5. Nature Observation
**Developing well:** Regular, detailed biological observations in the nature journal. Seasonal patterns noted. Questions recorded.
**Developing slowly:** Journal is sparse or focuses only on non-biological subjects.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Textbook Biology
If the student is learning about food chains from a worksheet instead of building one from organisms they have actually seen in their yard, you have lost the method. Start with observation. Always.

### Overcomplicating Body Systems
At this age, the student needs to know what the heart does, not the names of its chambers. What the lungs do, not the mechanism of gas exchange. Keep it functional and personal: "Your heart is beating right now. Feel it. That is pumping blood to every part of your body."

### Neglecting Microorganisms
The microorganism section is easy to skip. Do not. Making yogurt or sourdough, or even just discussing what makes bread rise, introduces the student to the vast world of invisible life. This foundation prevents the common misconception that all microorganisms are disease agents.
